Zero-Width Space (ZWSP): The Invisible Character That Breaks Your Code

Ever had this happen — the string on your screen reads just "ABC" or "123", but the code won't run or the URL won't post? Chances are you're staring at a "ghost character". This post covers one of the most common culprits — the zero-width space — and how to actually find it.
Zero-width space, also called the non-printing space or zero-width blank — a bit of a mouthful. Easier framing: it's an
Here's an example — an API URL with five zero-width spaces in it:
language-txt shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
And the cleaned-up version with the zero-width spaces removed:
To your eyes, and even pasted into VSCode, the two look identical.
So you'd assume they're the same URL.
Surely this is just a normal URL?
But this tiny invisible difference breaks the code. That's the zero-width space. It's not a bug — it's a real Unicode character:
How to find and remove them
Copying the code above into VSCode won't show you the difference. So here are a few ways to actually catch these things.
, and tick it. Now you can see the invisible whitespace.
doesn't catch it, there's another option — open VSCode settings, search for
VSCode will now highlight anything that isn't basic ASCII — and the zero-width spaces in your URL show up clearly.

ASCII is a character encoding covering English letters, digits, and punctuation. Chinese, Japanese, Korean — all outside ASCII. Zero-width space is also outside ASCII, which is why "Non Basic ASCII" catches it.
Another trick — paste the URL into a browser's address bar. The browser URL-encodes the zero-width space into
, so you'll suddenly see them everywhere:

Ever had a string that looks like plain "ABC" or "123" on screen but won't run as code or won't post as a URL? That's often a "ghost character" — specifically the zero-width space. This post covers what zero-width space (ZWSP) is, why it sneaks into your code, and how to find and remove it before it eats your afternoon.
